nodes.description: The product is delivered as a single Next.js workspace with four primary surfaces: notes, inspect, project, and chat. The main page owns most client state, active tabs, repo selection, console output, and cross-surface coordination, so it acts as the operational shell for the whole MVP. Evidence is concentrated in one large client component that loads status, project data, canvas data, and streaming run results.
nodes.title: Workspace Shell

nodes.description: The canvas is the editable architecture map where users create, update, delete, and connect notes that represent business areas and technical responsibilities. It supports direct manipulation on a visual board, stores linked files and symbols on each note, and can also be auto-generated from a prompt. The frontend renders notes with React Flow while the backend deduplicates generated titles, places nodes heuristically, and manages edges.
nodes.title: Architecture Canvas

nodes.description: The inspect surface is the read-only analysis area for structure browsing, symbol search, graph relationships, and impact analysis. It turns indexed graph data into navigable trees and ranked results so users can understand what a change might touch before editing. The backend exposes separate endpoints for symbols, relationships, structure, free-form queries, and impact heuristics, while the frontend binds them into one inspection workflow.
nodes.title: Inspection Workspace

nodes.description: The app tracks an active repository, shows recent projects, and reports whether a workspace already has files and canvas notes. This lets the product behave like a repo-oriented assistant instead of a generic chat tool, and it drives which canvas, conversations, and graph context are loaded. The service persists a lightweight project profile and derives recent-project lists and workspace status from the filesystem.
nodes.title: Project Selection

nodes.description: Each repository can have stored conversations that preserve user and assistant messages, titles, and timestamps. This gives planning, asking, and build runs short-term working memory tied to a specific codebase rather than a global session. The project service stores conversations in `.konceptura/conversations.json` and the frontend turns recent messages into conversation context for follow-up runs.
nodes.title: Conversation Memory

nodes.description: The workspace can read and write a repo-local `AGENTS.md`, treating it as editable operating guidance for future coding runs. This is a policy boundary because it changes how the assistant should behave inside a given repository without changing application code. The backend exposes explicit endpoints for fetching and persisting the file, and the frontend surfaces the document as part of project management.
nodes.title: Agent Instructions

nodes.description: Indexing is the workflow that turns a chosen repository into graph data available for search, structure browsing, and impact analysis. It runs `code-graph-rag` asynchronously, tracks job state, supports dry-run previews, and marks the indexed repo as active when successful. This is the product's ingestion boundary between raw source files and all later semantic features.
nodes.title: Repository Indexing

nodes.description: Graph querying is the semantic core that searches indexed symbols, resolves file paths, follows relationships, and builds a structural tree from Memgraph. It blends direct Cypher access with lightweight ranking heuristics so the UI can answer practical questions like 'where is this defined' and 'what files are likely impacted'. This service is the main business logic layer behind inspect and graph-assisted editing.
nodes.title: Code Graph Querying

nodes.description: The app offers three repo-scoped assistant modes: ask, plan, and build. These modes take user prompts plus semantic note context and recent conversation context, then either answer questions, draft an implementation plan, or make real code changes and refresh the architecture notes. A streaming endpoint emits phase updates and assistant text so the UI can behave like an active workspace rather than a blocking form submit.
nodes.title: Prompted Build And Q&A

nodes.description: Codex execution is the external editing engine that actually runs repository changes, plan generation, question answering, architecture-note generation, and commit message suggestions. Before editing, it can enrich the prompt with graph-derived impact hints and semantic workspace context; afterward it snapshots the repo to report changed files and command traces. This makes Codex an integration boundary rather than core product state.
nodes.title: Codex Execution

nodes.description: Canvas data is stored per repository under `.konceptura/canvas.json`, while a backend pointer keeps track of the currently active repo canvas. The persistence layer also manages a `.gitignore` policy so workspace metadata is retained locally without polluting the target project. This separates transient UI editing from durable architecture notes tied to a repository.
nodes.title: Canvas Persistence

nodes.description: Once Codex or the user changes a repo, the app can inspect git status, suggest a commit message from the diff, and create a commit. This closes the loop from analysis and editing to a concrete project checkpoint, keeping the product focused on real repository outcomes instead of draft-only assistance. The project service runs non-interactive git commands and the Codex service helps phrase the commit summary.
nodes.title: Git Commit Workflow

nodes.description: The system is explicitly local-first: configuration comes from `konceptura.toml`, Memgraph runs locally, code-graph-rag is invoked from the workspace, and the dev script starts backend and frontend together on fixed ports. Settings resolve binaries, repo paths, state files, and frontend origins, giving the rest of the app a stable local execution environment. This is the infrastructure boundary that all runtime services depend on.
nodes.title: Local Runtime Configuration
